file_name: Cerus Corporation - FIRST AMEND TO SUPPLY AND MANUFACTURING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise expressly provided in the applicable Statement of Work payment to Porex of undisputed fees shall be due [*] days following Cerus' receipt of the invoice submitted by Porex.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise expressly provided in the applicable Statement of Work, payment terms shall be determined by Cerus.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the predetermined payment timeframe for undisputed fees, giving Cerus complete control over payment terms. This introduces the potential for payment delays or unfair payment practices, conflicting with California's prompt payment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7107 requires contractors to pay subcontractors within a specific timeframe after receiving payment from the client. By removing the established payment due date, this contract language allows Cerus to delay payments indefinitely, potentially violating this prompt payment requirement if Porex is considered a subcontractor.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 7. Disciplinary Proceedings [7090 - 7124.6] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) 7107. Abandonment without legal excuse of any construction project or operation engaged in or undertaken by the licensee as a contractor constitutes a cause for disciplinary action. (Added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a predetermined payment timeframe and gives Cerus complete control over payment terms. This contradicts the prompt payment requirement mentioned in the Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly mention prompt payment for subcontractors, the Law Explanation explicitly states California Business and Professions Code Section 7107 requires contractors to pay subcontractors within a specific timeframe. The removal of the payment timeframe directly contradicts this requirement, as it allows for indefinite payment delays, making the contract non-compliant. The omission of the payment timeframe weakens the protection for Porex, creating a legal loophole. Because the Law Explanation provides a legal basis for prompt payment, removing the payment terms opens the door for non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: For disputed invoices or the disputed portion of an invoice, Cerus shall use reasonable efforts to provide to Porex, in writing, within [*] days, a description of the disputed amounts. Cerus and Porex shall negotiate in a timely, good faith manner to resolve billing queries.
perturbation.changed_text: For disputed invoices or the disputed portion of an invoice, Cerus shall provide a description of the disputed amounts. Cerus and Porex shall negotiate to resolve billing queries.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'reasonable efforts', 'in writing', 'within [*] days' and 'in a timely, good faith manner' weakens the obligation for Cerus to provide a prompt and documented explanation for disputed invoice amounts. This introduces ambiguity and the potential for delayed or insufficient communication regarding disputed invoices, potentially violating California's implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1655 implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the requirement for 'reasonable efforts,' a written explanation, a specific timeframe, and 'good faith manner', the amended language allows Cerus to handle invoice disputes arbitrarily, potentially violating this implied covenant by preventing Porex from receiving timely and fair resolution of billing issues.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1655. Stipulations which are necessary to make a contract reasonable, or conformable to usage, are implied, in respect to matters concerning which the contract manifests no contrary intention. (Enacted 1872.)

perturbation.justification: The original contract included specific obligations to ensure good faith and timely resolution of disputes. By removing 'reasonable efforts,' 'in writing,' 'within [*] days,' and 'in a timely, good faith manner,' the revised text weakens the duty for Cerus to provide a prompt and documented explanation for disputed invoice amounts, creating potential for delayed or insufficient communication. The Law Explanation posits that California Civil Code § 1655 implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2, both quoting California Civil Code § 1655, state that stipulations necessary to make a contract reasonable or conformable to usage are implied, unless the contract explicitly states otherwise. Therefore, removing explicit requirements of good faith introduces ambiguity that can make the contract unreasonable. The omission of these requirements directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith.
